title: Critical points in a Linked List

content.problemStatement:
Given the head of a linked list, Find the number of critical points. (The starting and end are not considered critical points).

Local minima or maxima are called critical points.

A Node is called a local minima if both next and previous elements are greater than the current element.

A Node is called a local maxima if both next and previous elements are smaller than the current element.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the linked list. The second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the node values in order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of critical points (local minima or maxima) in the list. The first and last nodes are never counted as critical points.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The node with value 1 at position 7 is a local minimum (neighbors 5 and 3). The node with value 5 at position 6 is a local maximum (neighbors 3 and 1). Hence there are 2 critical points.
Critical nodes are at positions 3 (value 3, a maximum), 5 (value 1, a minimum) and 6 (value 3, a maximum). Total = 3.
All values are equal, so no node is strictly greater or smaller than both neighbours. Hence 0 critical points.

solution.approach: Even though the problem mentions a linked list, the values can be processed as an array because only neighbour relationships matter. For each test case we read the N values into an array. We then iterate from the second element to the second‑last element (indices 1 to N‑2). A position i is a critical point if arr[i] is strictly greater than both neighbours (local maximum) or strictly smaller than both neighbours (local minimum). We count such positions and output the count. The algorithm is O(N) time and O(1) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the list.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space (besides the input array).
